title: Human induced pluripotent stem cell derived bronchial epithelium to model primary ciliary dyskinesia
author: J. De Vos
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSC) are obtained by reprogramming differentiated cells into pluripotency. They can differentiate in vitro into numerous, if not every, human cell type and are therefore a promising source of cells for normal and diseased organ modelling. As a proof of concept that iPSC can model lung disease, we aimed to reproduce in vitro primary ciliary dyskinesia, a genetic disease that causes defects in the action of cilia of the respiratory tract. We reprogrammed human skin fibroblasts from a patient with primary ciliary dyskinesia using Sendai viruses expressing OCT4, SOX2, c-MYC and KLF4. Several iPSC clones were amplified, characterized and validated. These diseased iPSC were then differentiated into cells of the lung lineage by a 4 steps protocol. Definitive endoderm was obtained by adding Activin A in low serum concentration conditions. Anterior foregut endoderm was then induced by blocking TGF and BMP signaling, and lung progenitor cells by exposure to BMP4 and FGF10. Cells were further cultured in air liquid interface (ALI) conditions to promote the formation of bronchial epithelium. Analysis of the final epithelium shows the expression of several mRNAs from the airway lineage, such as NKX2.1, FOXJ1, MUC5A, and SFPTC. These results demonstrate the production of bronchial epithelial cells and lung cells from iPSC. Further characterization of this epithelium is underway to measure the extent of the differentiation, the proportion of each cell type obtained and the cilia activity.
